title: Star Wars: The Thrawn Trilogy

'''''Star Wars: The Thrawn Trilogy''''' is a [[Star Wars Legends|Legends]] adult-fiction novel series from author [[Timothy Zahn]]. It consists of ''[[Heir to the Empire]]'' ([[1991]]), ''[[Dark Force Rising]]'' ([[1992]]), and ''[[The Last Command]]'' ([[1993]]), which were published by [[Bantam Spectra]].

The trilogy is set in [[9 ABY/Legends|9 ABY]], five [[Standard year/Legends|years]] after the events of [[Star Wars: Episode VI Return of the Jedi|''Star Wars'': Episode VI ''Return of the Jedi'']], and it chronicles the [[Thrawn campaign|campaign]] of the eponymous [[Grand Admiral/Legends|Grand Admiral]] [[Mitth'raw'nuruodo/Legends|Thrawn]].

==Contents==
*''[[Heir to the Empire]]'' {{C|[[May]] [[1991]]}}<ref name="Year By Year" />
*[[Heir to the Empire (unabridged audiobook)|''Heir to the Empire'' audiobook]] {{C|[[September]] [[2011]]}}<ref name="Year By Year" />
*''[[Dark Force Rising]]'' {{C|[[May]] [[1992]]}}<ref name="Essential Reader's Companion" />
*[[Dark Force Rising (unabridged audiobook)|''Dark Force Rising'' audiobook]] {{C|[[May]] [[1992]]}}<ref name="DFR Cassette">{{RandomHouseOld|url=catalog/display.pperl?0553470558|text=Star Wars: Dark Force Rising}}</ref>
*''[[The Last Command]]'' {{C|[[April]] [[1993]]}}<ref name="Essential Reader's Companion" />
*[[The Last Command (unabridged audiobook)|''The Last Command'' audiobook]] {{C|[[April]] [[1993]]}}<ref name="TLC Cassette">{{RandomHouseOld|url=catalog/display.pperl?0553471570|text=Star Wars: The Last Command}}</ref>

==Plot summary==
The [[Galactic Empire/Legends|Empire]] has withered to a quarter of its former size as the [[New Republic/Legends|New Republic]] continues to consolidate its hold on [[The galaxy/Legends|the galaxy]]. From the [[Unknown Regions/Legends|Unknown Regions]] emerges a new Imperial [[Commander/Legends|commander]], a mysterious tactical genius named [[Grand Admiral/Legends|Grand Admiral]] [[Mitth'raw'nuruodo/Legends|Thrawn]], with an elaborate and grandiose plan to destroy the New Republic. Commanding the [[Imperial II-class Star Destroyer/Legends|Star Destroyer]] [[Chimaera/Legends|''Chimaera'']] with [[Captain/Legends|Captain]] [[Gilad Pellaeon/Legends|Pellaeon]], Thrawn begins an intricate stratagem built form pieces of information gleaned from across the galaxy. From a data raid in the [[Obroa-skai system/Legends|Obroa-skai system]], he obtains the locations of one of the [[Palpatine/Legends|Emperor's]] secret storehouses: [[Mount Tantiss/Legends|Mount Tantiss]] on [[Wayland/Legends|Wayland]]. From the [[Forest/Legends|forests]] of [[Myrkr/Legends|Myrkr]], he procures a peculiar [[Force resistance|Force-repelling]] creature called an [[Ysalamiri/Legends|ysalamir]]. With an ysalamir carried in a specialized backpack, Thrawn arrives on Wayland immune to the [[Force power/Legends|Force abilities]] of [[Joruus C'Baoth]], the mad [[Jedi Master/Legends|Jedi]] [[Cloning/Legends|clone]] guarding the Emperor's safehouse. C'baoth figures into Thrawn's plans – the loyal [[Force-sensitive/Legends|Force-sensitive]] can help coordinate Thrawn's fighting forces to a supernatural degree. The Grand Admiral offers the insane Jedi the promise of new apprentices, since [[Luke Skywalker/Legends|Luke Skywalker]] endeavors to rebuild the [[Jedi Order/Legends|Jedi Order]]. 

On a New Republic [[Diplomat/Legends|diplomatic]] mission to [[Bimmisaari/Legends|Bimmisaari]], shadowy [[Alien/Legends|alien]] assailants – Thrawn's [[Noghri Death Commando|Noghri death commandos]] – attack Luke and [[Leia Organa Solo/Legends|Leia]] in a failed kidnapping attempt. Wary of such near misses, a [[Reproduction/Legends|pregnant]] Leia hides out on [[Kashyyyk/Legends|Kashyyyk]] with [[Chewbacca/Legends|Chewbacca]] and his fellow [[Wookiee/Legends|Wookiees]] as protectors. They defend her from another strike, capturing one of the [[Noghri/Legends|Noghri]], [[Khabarakh]], in the process. Leia is stunned when the Noghri proclaims fealty to her upon discovering – through his powerful sense of smell – that she is biologically related to [[Anakin Skywalker/Legends|Darth Vader]]. The Noghri venerate Vader as a savior. Elsewhere in the galaxy, when Luke narrowly escapes an Imperial ambush, he is found stranded in [[Realspace/Legends|space]] by [[Smuggler/Legends|smugglers]] [[Talon Karrde]] and [[Mara Jade Skywalker|Mara Jade]]. Mara is a tight-lipped, intensely focused [[Gender/Legends|woman]] who has been with [[Smugglers' Alliance|Karrde's organization]] for only six [[Standard month/Legends|months]]. She seemingly sensed Luke's presence in interstellar space and ehxibits an intense hatred of the [[Jedi/Legends|Jedi]]. Talon, realizing that Luke may be of great value, keeps him locked up in a supply shed at his [[Myrkr Base|headquarters on Myrkr]], using the Force-blocking power of the ysalamiri against him.

[[Han Solo/Legends|Han Solo]] and [[Lando Calrissian/Legends|Lando Calrissian]] attempt to connect with Karrde's organization, and the courteous smuggler hosts them at Myrkr, all the while keeping Luke's [[Prison/Legends|imprisonment]] a secret from them. Luke escapes, however, and Mara gives pursuit. The two are forced to work side by side to survive the dangers of the [[Great Northern Forest|forests]]. Here Luke discovers that Mara was once an [[Emperor's Hand/Legends|Emperor's Hand]] and that the [[Death/Legends|death]] Palpatine destroyed Mara's life. Thrawn's next major move in his campaign against the New Republic is an attack on the bustling [[Sluis Van Shipyards|shipyards of Sluis Van]]. Using specially modified [[Mining/Legends|mining]] equipment [[Thief/Legends|stolen]] from a complex owned by Lando Calrissian, Thrawn's intent is not to destroy the [[New Republic Defense Fleet/Legends|New Republic fleet]] – but rather to ''steal it''. The mining equipment cuts into the control systems of the unscrewed [[Starship/Legends|ships]], allowing Thrawn's [[Soldier/Legends|troops]] to commandeer the vessels. Though the New Republic manages to thwart Thrawn's theft before he can complete it, the victory is costly as he leaves many vessels inoperable.

Compounding the New Republic's difficulties are feats of [[Politics/Legends|political]] overreach and backstabbing from New Republic [[Councillor/Legends|councilor]] [[Borsk Fey'la]]. Hoping to more firmly entrench [[Bothan/Legends|Bothan]] political power at the expense of the rival [[Mon Calamari/Legends|Mon Calamari]], Fey'la pounces upon evidence planted by the Empire asserting that [[Admiral/Legends|Admiral]] [[Gial Ackbar/Legends|Ackbar]] was guilty of embezzlement. Solo and Calrissian embark on a private investigation into these Bothan affairs, which leads them to [[New Cov]]. There allies of Fey'la have been helping [[Garm Bel Iblis/Legends|Garm Bel Iblis]] fight his own private war against the Empire. Bel Iblis, a former [[Corellian/Legends|Corellian]] [[Senator/Legends|Senator]] and cofounder of the [[Alliance to Restore the Republic/Legends|Rebel Alliance]], has a falling out with [[Mon Mothma/Legends|Mon Mothma]]. He never joined the New Republic, opting to [[Garm Bel Iblis's movement|wage war on his own]]. He has gradually come to realize that his pride and his refusal to cooperate have kept him from better helping the cause of freedom in the galaxy.

While with Bel Iblis, Han and Lando find evidence that some of the Corellian Senator [[Warship/Legends|warships]] were [[Dreadnaught-class heavy cruiser/Legends|Dreadnaughts]] from the fabled [[Katana fleet|''Katana'' fleet]]. The two-hundred-ship-strong task force vanished into [[Hyperspace/Legends|hyperspace]] on its maiden voyage during the final decades of the [[Galactic Republic/Legends|Galactic Republic]] and has been the subject of [[Treasure hunting/Legends|treasure-seeking]] spacer legends ever since. Finding the fleet's heavily armed and armored warships would represent a huge boon to either the Empire or the New Republic. The ever resourceful Talon Karrde has a lead on the ships. He was once a protégé [[Jorj Car'das]], a well-informed smuggler who knew of the fleet's location. Leia voyages to [[Honoghr/Legends|Honoghr]], the [[Poison/Legends|poisoned]] [[Planet/Legends|homeworld]] of the Noghri, with her captive would-be [[Assassin/Legends|assassin]], Khabarakh. The honor-bound Noghri keep her hidden on the planet, despite visiting Imperials. Leia learns of the Noghri history: how their planet was poisoned during the [[Clone Wars/Legends|Clone Wars]] by a starship crash, and how Darth Vader engineered a plan to save the planet's crippled agriculture to keep it habitable. Leia's inspection of the [[Decon droid|decon droids]] tasked with maintaining Honoghr's crops reveals a deep betrayal – the droids are poisoning the planet just enough to keep the Noghri subservient to the Empire.

Meanwhile, Luke follows the rumors of a [[Jedi Knight/Legends|Jedi Knight]] ruling on distant [[Jomark/Legends|Jomark]] and finds Joruus C'baoth there. C'baoth takes Skywalker under his wing, teaching Luke his distressing authoritarian view of Jedi in society. Luke comes to realize that Joruus is not touched by the [[Dark side of the Force/Legends|dark side]], but also insane. The hunt for the Katana fleet leads to a standoff between the New Republic and Imperial forces in the depths of space. The Empire succeeds in capturing most of the Dreadnaughts, while the New Republic discovers something just as disturbing – the [[Stormtrooper/Legends|stormtroopers]] who attacked them during the battle were freshly produced clones. Thrawn has the technology to build an [[Army/Legends|army]], and a new era of clone wars may fall upon the galaxy. At Mount Tantiss, Thrawn uses captured [[Spaarti cloning cylinder]] to rapidly produce clones. He uses ysalamiri in the cloning process to prevent the "clone madness" inherent in such fast production. Thrawn has also recovered a prototype [[Cloaking device/Legends|cloaking device]], which he uses to cleverly place [[Coruscant/Legends|Coruscant]] under siege. Thrawn has cloaked an unknown number of [[Asteroid/Legends|asteroids]] and released them above the planet's surface, thus preventing the capital from lowering its [[Planetary shield/Legends|shields]] for fear of a devastating asteroid strike. 

While the New Republic is pinned beneath their own shields, Leia Organa gives birth to twins – [[Darth Caedus|Jacen]] and [[Jaina Solo Fel|Jaina Solo]]. Even as infants, they exhibit strength in [[The Force/Legends|the Force]]. Rallying against Thrawn, and with intelligence offered by Mara Jade, the New Republic heroes infiltrate the Mount Tantiss storehouse on Wayland. Within a throne room built for the Emperor inside the mountain, the heroes are attacked by Joruus C'baoth. The Jedi madman releases a horrible [[Weapon/Legends|weapon]] against Luke – a [[Luuke Skywalker|clone]] created from Skywalker's severed hand, recovered from [[Bespin/Legends|Bespin]] [[Standard year/Legends|years]] ago. In the end, Mara strikes down this duplicate. In killing an avatar of Skywalker, Mara releases some of her long-standing animosity toward Luke. Mara also succeeds in killing C'baoth, and [[Explosive charge/Legends|explosives]] tear through the [[Mountain/Legends|mountain]], destroying Thrawn's cloning facility. At [[Battle of Bilbringi (9 ABY)|Bilgringi]], site of an assault by Thrawn, his fleet is opposed by a coalition of smuggler vessels organized by Talon Karrde as well as New Republic space forces, including [[Rogue Squadron/Legends|Rogue Squadron]]. Though Thrawn's tactical cunning once again gives him the advantage, he fails to predict once crucial attack. His Noghri [[Bodyguard/Legends|bodyguard]], [[Rukh/Legends|Rukh]], is now aware of the Imperial deception on his home world. He stabs Thrawn in the chest, killing the Imperial warlord. Without Thrawn to steer the assault, the Imperial advance collapses, and Pellaeon calls for a retreat.

==Continuity==
Perhaps the most influential work of the [[Star Wars Legends|Expanded Universe]], ''The Thrawn Trilogy'' successfully relaunched ''[[Star Wars]]'' published storytelling in [[1991]] and introduced characters such as [[Grand Admiral/Legends|Grand Admiral]] [[Mitth'raw'nuruodo/Legends|Thrawn]], [[Talon Karrde]], [[Mara Jade Skywalker|Mara Jade]], [[Garm Bel Iblis/Legends|Garm Bel Iblis]], [[Borsk Fey'la]], [[Darth Caedus|Jacen]] and [[Jaina Solo Fel|Jaina Solo]], and [[Gilad Pellaeon/Legends|Gilad Pellaeon]] into the ''Star Wars'' pantheon. These heroes and villains would have long careers in the books that followed. The first novel of the cycle, ''[[Heir to the Empire]]'', was honored with a [[Heir to the Empire: The 20th Anniversary Edition|twentieth anniversary edition]] in [[2011]], which included many [[Heir to the Empire: Behind the Scenes - an Expanded Universe Is Born|behind the scenes annotations]] about the creation of the story. It was ''Heir'' that truly began building the Expanded Universe. Before that, ''Star Wars'' storytelling was spread across different media, without a determined mandate to construct a consistent setting. As a result, works published from [[1977]] to [[1990]] would often contradict one another. With ''Heir'', [[Lucasfilm]] asked [[Timothy Zahn|Zahn]] to coordinate his story with the world building being undertaken by the ''[[Star Wars: The Roleplaying Game]]'' publisher [[West End Games]], thus ensuring his work fit into a larger whole. The collective title ''The Thrawn Trilogy'' did not come into effect until [[1996]]. Before that, these three books were simply an untitled "3-Book Cycle," and for a time were branded as The Empire Trilogy. This trilogy underwent [[Star Wars: The Thrawn Trilogy (hardcover)|comic adaptations]] by [[Dark Horse Comics|Dark Horse]] in [[1995]]-[[1998|98]], resulting in three six-issue series devoted to this story.<ref name="Essential Reader's Companion" /> 

When the first installment of the trilogy, ''Heir to the Empire'' was released in 1991, ''Lucasfilm Fan Club Magazine'' clarified in the same year that although the book was licensed and approved by Lucasfilm, ''Heir to the Empire'' is not [[George Lucas]]' story, nor the official continuation of the saga, but, rather, a story of Timothy Zahn's own imagination.<ref name="The Lucasfilm Fan Club Magazine 14">[[The Lucasfilm Fan Club Magazine 14|''The Lucasfilm Fan Club Magazine'' 14]]</ref> The sourcebooks released for the novels in the following years also noted that all products taking place after [[Star Wars: Episode VI Return of the Jedi|''Star Wars VI: Return of the Jedi'']], are the author's vision of what may have happened, and not the true fate of the characters.<ref name="Heir to the Empire Sourcebook">''[[Heir to the Empire Sourcebook]]'' (Disclaimer)</ref><ref name="Dark Force Rising Sourcebook">''[[Dark Force Rising Sourcebook]]'' (Disclaimer)</ref><ref name="The Last Command Sourcebook">''[[The Last Command Sourcebook]]'' (Disclaimer)</ref> In 1994, [[Howard Roffman]], head of [[Lucas Licensing]] clarified, the books following the events of Episode VI were never intended to be the story of the planned [[Sequel trilogy|Episode VII-IX]].<ref name="The Lucasfilm Fan Club Magazine 22">[[The Lucasfilm Fan Club Magazine 22|''The Lucasfilm Fan Club Magazine'' 22]]</ref> In ''[[A Guide to the Star Wars Universe, Second Edition, Revised and Expanded]]'', published in [[1994]], the trilogy, alongside with books such as ''[[Splinter of the Mind's Eye]]'', was noted to be ''“officially licensed source that may or may not agree with George Lucas' vision of the Star Wars galaxy”'' as opposed to ''“original Lucasfilm source.”''<ref name="A Guide to the Star Wars Universe, Second Edition, Revised and Expanded">''[[A Guide to the Star Wars Universe, Second Edition, Revised and Expanded]]''</ref> Nevertheless, some continued to entertain the idea that the ''Thrawn Trilogy'' is the official continuation of Lucas' saga or it was based on Lucas' plans for Episode VII-IX, something that was addressed by [[Pablo Hidalgo]] in the [[Star Wars Insider 77|77th issue of ''Star Wars Insider'']] in [[2004]]: ''"That's not the case. Those works are the creation of their respective authors with the guidance of editors at Lucas Licensing. They are not, nor ever were, meant to be George Lucas' definitive vision of what happens next."''<ref name="Star Wars Insider 77">[[Star Wars Insider 77|''Star Wars Insider'' 77]]</ref> After George Lucas started to work on the sequel trilogy and sold Lucasfilm to [[The Walt Disney Company|Disney]] in [[2012]], the novels alongside other Expanded Universe material was set into a separate continuity and rebranded as Legends in [[2014]].<ref name="End of EU">{{SW|url=news/the-legendary-star-wars-expanded-universe-turns-a-new-page|text=The Legendary Star Wars Expanded Universe Turns a New Page}}</ref>

Numerous mentions are made in the trilogy to the [[Clone Wars/Legends|Clone Wars]] that are contradicted by later portrayals in [[Star Wars: Episode II Attack of the Clones|Episode II]] and the [[Star Wars: The Clone Wars|''Clone Wars'' TV show]] (e.g., that the Clone Wars were waged by "the clone masters" in an attempt to "roll over [[The galaxy/Legends|the galaxy]]"). Additionally, the [[Katana fleet|Dark Force]] (or ''Katana'' fleet) is said to have been created by the [[Galactic Republic/Legends|Republic]] to showcase their military might prior to the Clone Wars, which is contradicted by Episode II, where the Republic explicitly refuses to have a standing military prior to the purchase of the [[Grand Army of the Republic/Legends|Grand Army of the Republic]] from the [[Kaminoan/Legends|Kaminoans]] at the onset of the Clone Wars. The trilogy follows the [[Thrawn campaign|campaign]] of [[Galactic Empire/Legends|Imperial]] Grand Admiral Thrawn, from the [[destruction of the Elomin task force]] to the [[Battle of Bilbringi (9 ABY)|Battle of Bilbringi]]. It also recounts the efforts of the [[Dark Jedi/Legends|Dark Jedi]] [[Joruus C'baoth]] and his efforts to claim [[Luke Skywalker/Legends|Luke Skywalker]], [[Leia Organa Solo/Legends|Leia Organa Solo]], and her [[Solo twins|children]] as his apprentices. In addition, the Imperial capital world, [[Coruscant/Legends|Coruscant]], was given its name by the trilogy and would later feature in the [[Prequel trilogy|prequel films]].

A soundtrack for the trilogy had been in discussion by [[Robert Townson]], the vice president of [[Wikipedia:Varèse Sarabande|Varèse Sarabande]], but did not go any further.<ref name="Secrets">''[[The Secrets of Star Wars: Shadows of the Empire]]''</ref>
